title: One Vote from Each Person; One Vote through Each Person!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, non-text scan fragments, punctuation, and word-break errors have been repaired. Names, campaign figures, and the eight-million-member calculation are preserved. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: My election tour has been scheduled and announced from Coimbatore on 5 April to Chennai on the twenty-first. Udanpirappugal who meet me daily ask anxiously whether my health can bear it and whether I can travel hundreds of kilometres each day seated in a van. When have I ever worried about my health?
Even before my tour begins, our General Secretary, the Professor, has started campaigning and attended several programmes in Chennai, Sriperumbudur and Dharmapuri districts over the past few days.

There is no need even to ask about Treasurer Thalapathi Stalin. He has already completed campaigning in twenty constituencies. Friends tell me in person and by telephone about his labour and his meetings with voters. Some say: “It is just as you used to speak with emotion and awakening, labour without rest by day and night, remain awake through the night in contact with Udanpirappugal, learn local conditions, encourage and advise them.” When I hear this, memories of my earlier days play across my heart.

Principal Secretary Arcot Veerasamy said he would accompany me, but considering his health I asked him not to. Deputy General Secretary Duraimurugan will travel with me. Campaign programmes have also been announced for Deputy General Secretaries Sarguna Pandian and V.P. Duraisamy and leading speakers including Kanimozhi MP, A. Rahman Khan, Tiruchi Siva, Bishop Ezra Sargunam, Vagai Chandrasekar, Khushbu Sundar, Cumbum Selvendran, Professor Sabapathy Mohan, Noorjahan Begum, Sankari Narayanan, Pulavar Indira Kumari, Pudukkottai Vijaya, Thanjai Kootharasan, V.P. Rajan, Nellikuppam Pugazhenthi, Dindigul Leoni, Kumarimuthu, Vasu Vikram and Inigo Irudayaraj.
Women's Wing members including Vasanthi Stanley and Vijaya Thayanban met me and asked with due right whether they too should not join the tour, requesting campaign arrangements.

Allied-party leaders and front-line workers are campaigning without pause. Dravidar Kazhagam President, Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani, has drawn up a month-long tour. Programmes are arranged for Professors Suba Veerapandian, Kader Mohideen and Jawahirullah.

Thol. Thirumavalavan and Dr Krishnasamy seek votes in their constituencies and campaign elsewhere. Writer Manushya Puthiran is campaigning for us. Volunteers and social movements, particularly persons with disabilities, transgender people, minorities, teachers' associations, lawyers, weavers, farmers, fishers, students, workers, government employees and People's Welfare Workers, are all contributing to the Democratic Progressive Alliance's victory.
You will have seen on television the firm explanations and news presented at our meetings.

Meanwhile, for the AIADMK, one leader alone struggles pitiably. Carrying pages of a prepared speech, she flies by “helicopter,” climbs the stage without fatigue, allows no one else to speak, will not even let the candidate ask for votes or sit, seats innocent people brought there under the blazing sun without drinking water, and repeatedly asks, “Will you do it? Will you?” They give no answer, but sit wondering whether the promise made when they were brought will be kept and when they will be allowed to leave. One television channel broadcasts the same speech twenty-four hours a day, and CDs are prepared for every constituency. That is what happens there!

At Jayalalithaa's meetings there is only a chronicle of abuse. Half the time is spent listing supposed betrayals by the Congress-led Central government and accusing the DMK of assisting them. The other half is spent claiming credit for things she did not do, appropriating DMK achievements and opening bundles of falsehood.

She does not explain what her government did for Tamil Nadu in three years or what became of the announcements she alone read daily under Assembly Rule 110, denying other Ministers the chance and securing praise and publicity from allied leaders.

What happened to the Vandalur bus terminus, free bus passes for senior citizens, the endlessly promised Monorail, twenty litres of purified water monthly for every family, lower prices, a power-cut-free surplus state, 560,000 new jobs and 20,000 factories? Were these delivered? Why not? Should she not answer at her meetings?

Should she not tell the people why, over three years, she obstructed and closed achievement after achievement of the preceding DMK government? Why did her government block infrastructure projects the Centre itself offered Tamil Nadu? Why did it first declare the Sethusamudram Project necessary and then ask the Supreme Court to reject it?
Instead of explaining, if Jayalalithaa throws dust by listing alleged Central-government corruption, opposition parties will naturally list her corruption.
You will have seen that I answer each accusation through my letters to Udanpirappugal. I have failed to explain none of her questions, while she has answered none of ours.

First she falsely said we did not provide reservation to Muslims. The next day I gave the date on which the DMK government ordered 3.5 per cent separate reservation and cited Muslim leaders' denials. She abandoned that and falsely denied sending people to kar seva. I answered with newspaper reports. She moved on again. In Coimbatore she falsely said investment was not leaving Tamil Nadu and that Stalin and I lied. I answered that in detail too. You will have read it.

Let them campaign in their manner; we shall continue in ours. Both parties have issued manifestos. Reading them reveals the difference. As Tamilar Thalaivar Veeramani put it, the DMK manifesto is `isai`, music; the AIADMK manifesto is `vasai`, abuse.

Before beginning my tour, I want to tell you a few things. Most newspapers now concentrate on throwing stones at us because of government full-page advertisements, fear of ruling-party intimidation and the politics unleashed upon them. If even a small incident occurs in the DMK, they magnify it a hundredfold, give it eyes and ears and publish it prominently.

Why? They have no faith in the Dravidian movement's distinctive identities of people and language; more than disbelief, they have an allergy to them. They highlight reports that some oppose a constituency candidate or have not worked, hoping to reduce our enthusiasm.

Instead of worrying about them, why should we give such reports any basis? If we prepare ourselves properly, how can others create conflict among us? I have said and written before: “Water struck by water does not separate.” Everyone may seek a constituency opportunity. Once one person receives it and another awaits a future occasion, all work together for the chosen candidate.

For it is not merely an individual contesting there. The DMK itself contests. Perarignar Anna, the great force who founded the movement and moves us each day, is the candidate. I humbly and affectionately ask every comrade to work with that thought.

Former Thanjavur Municipal Chairman and Rules Amendment Committee member C. Iraivan wrote to me yesterday. There are now eight million DMK members, he said, all included in the Lok Sabha electoral rolls. Every one must vote for our alliance. Each should also take one non-member and secure that person's vote for our candidate. If all do so properly, we gain another eight million votes. Together, the DMK alone can easily deliver sixteen million votes to our alliance in Tamil Nadu. If these votes come, a tremendous victory is certain.

Udanpirappē, Iraivan's calculation seems right. My affectionate request to every member is this: approach at least one person who is not a member and bring that person to vote for our alliance. Do not think, “Our elder brother asked for one, so one is enough.” One is the minimum; add as many votes as you can.
From today, every Udanpirappu must work without forgetting: one vote from each person; one vote through each person. Victory is ours! I leave today to see your dear face.
With affection,
M.K.
5 April 2014
